speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you'll tell us when we have speed?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were telling us that after about seven weeks in Auschwitz, you became ill with the measles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So there were actually two epidemics in camp. The first one was a very mild scarlet fever epidemic, and they took people away and nobody came back. So we knew where they went. So when they took me away with the measles, my friends thought that I go to the same destination. So they cried. And-- but there was  nothing they could do, because measles were like very visible all over my body, and they couldn't hide me. I was taken to a different camp, a small camp. Not where the main hospital was. But I knew through the grapevine that my cousin, who is 17 years older than I am, a practicing physician in private life, she was in this camp, working in this hospital, as a doctor-- a prisoner, of course-- as a doctor. The only reason she survived was because when that transport arrived from a different city, also in Transylvania, but a larger city, a university town, they asked doctors to stand aside when they arrived at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as a good, law-abiding citizen, as we all were, she stood aside. Her only child, a little boy, who turned 7 years old during that journey to Auschwitz, went with my cousin's parents, with the boy's grandparents, to the left. If they didn't need doctors that day, of course, she would have gone with her child to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got to the hospital, the pediatrician, also from the same city where my cousin was from, examined me. And I was-- I told her that my cousin is there. So she came running, and she was relieved that at that point they didn't take people with measles to the gas chamber. There was already a room established for people who had the measles. Eventually there was a room for people with scarlet fever, too. Not while I was there at the hospital. So some of these people might have also survived. My-- it was pretty-- I was-- I was pretty ill, because at 18, I think, that-- it takes a lot out of you. You're older than you should be when you have the measles. And after the disease itself was over, I was running a high fever for weeks. They didn't actually know why, because, of course, we didn't have x-ray machines or nothing to establish what the route of sickness was. They suspected that it was some kind of a secondary infection. It could have been pulmonary or they didn't know exactly. But I was running a really high fever. My cousin kept me working at this hospital as a nurse, a nurse's aid, whatever you want to call that. Obviously the hospitals weren't filled with people who really needed medical attention, except for the couple of the epidemics. They were just there for show. So I suppose they never knew what organization might force its way to Auschwitz one day, and they could just shut up the gas chamber for a day and show them around the hospital. The same way as the postcards, I assume. I was working actually in a ward where the scarlet fever people were. And it was a very, very mild epidemic. So they were basically doing very well, all of them. They had very slight fever at the beginning, and then they were OK. Our duties were to sweep the room, which at the beginning, I think, the spoiled little brat I was, I think I never had a broom in my hand before. So somebody always got off the bed, and older person, and showed me how to do it. But eventually I did learn that. And I also learned how to bandage pretty well with paper bandages. That was the only thing that we had. Sometimes we would get unsorted medicine from transport. This way we got-- I remember-- some calcium tablets. And my cousin gave me handfuls of calcium tablets, hoping that it might help me get in a little better shape, because I was in pretty miserable shape with all that fever and everything. In this hospital, we had it a little better physically than outside. We worked at-- first of all, instead of seven people sleeping on a-- on top of cot, two of us slept in a single cot, which was a major improvement. We did not stand roll calls, which was also not only for the fact that we didn't have to get up at 3 o'clock in the morning, but also that we didn't feel that our life is in danger every day by being selected. There's absolutely no question about it that if my cousin wouldn't have-- wouldn't have kept me in this hospital, I would not have survived the first selection. I never had any extra weight. I lost some during the first seven weeks. I lost more with the measles and the high fever. But Dr. Mengele came every single day and he inspected every room every single day. And we had to report to him as to how many prisoners-- we were not allowed to say people-- we had in each room. The only way I could survive, even in the hospital, was that when I had a very high fever, and I couldn't be working, they woke up the night nurse and sent her on the floor while Mengele was inspecting. And they told him that I'm the night nurse sleeping in the nurses' room, because only one person was allowed to sleep during the day, and that was the one night nurse in the whole hospital. So that's the way I survived. My cousin was really wonderful. She was very brave. I assume losing her only child, life didn't matter a lot to her at that time. She was the only person who even dared sort of argue, maybe, with Mengele, who didn't just submit to his requests. Eventually everybody had to, but she tried her best. She worked instead of me when I was sick. Her sister was there, too, who is closer to my age. She was sick a lot. She did her job, too. She was stealing potatoes for the patients in the hospital. And she did really everything she could. She worked a lot. I suppose she really buried her sorrow and her terrible sadness, losing her only child in work every day. Also she did something that would have cost her life, every time she did it. And I didn't know until later when we came back and she told me. There were women who were at the very beginning of their pregnancy, maybe they even didn't know that they're pregnant, when they arrived at Auschwitz, and they found out later. Performing an abortion was risking your life. It was illegal. It was not allowed. It was definitely punishable by death. She did a lot of those under the worst circumstances, in total secret, risking her life every time she did it, but trying to save a life every time she would do it. Working in the hospital, our physical comforts, of course, were a little better. But we paid dearly in other ways. Dr. Mengele came every single day, and at least once or twice a week, he would order us how many people he wants for the gas chambers. He would do the selection. But we who worked there, we had to guide those people out to the waiting trucks. And the longer we worked there, the more of us and the more of them knew where they're going and what is happening. Also sometimes we had dead bodies. Not too often, but we did have some. We would then have to put those dead bodies on the trucks, more often than not with the people who are alive, to be taken to the same place. Of course, I remember when first I had to do it, I came back, and I couldn't eat my little piece of bread for dinner. But I suppose your spirit to survive, even subconsciously, is stronger than anything else. After awhile, you just wash your hand as well as you can, and you eat your bread, because hunger is probably the strongest emotion, and strongest feeling that you have. And even if you don't know that you want to-- but basically, you do want to survive. I almost had a chance to save a life once, but it didn't work out. One day the hospital was basically three barracks, and they were connected by one hallway, the three barracks. And one day somebody came running to me and said that somebody who I know from my hometown sent for me. She was in the third barrack, that they took a group of 30 people from a different camp, ready to be taken to the gas chamber. I went there and this was a girl who I used to go to Sunday School with in junior high school. She wasn't a close friend, but I knew her from junior high school. And she came to me, and she hugged me, and she cried. She knew what her destiny is going to be. But I guess she didn't know that her mother going to the left is not alive anymore when they arrived. So she asked me if I see her mother, if I survived to tell her mother what happened to her. Of course, I felt horrible. These people were not sick. I mean, she really had-- there was nothing wrong with her except the same thing that was wrong with all of us, being undernourished and such. And I went to my cousin, and I asked her if there is anything we could do. It just so happened that there was a little girl who was in coma already, for a couple of days that Mengele didn't know about. Actually the day they were taken, she was dead already. So the-- not my cousin, but another doctor who was sort of higher up in the administration of the hospital, a Polish lady, came with me. And I was standing behind this girl, and she came to this room where they were held. And she said, well, I'm going to check you out, all of you. And she pointed at her, and said, come on, you'll be the first one. And we took her out. It was a mixed feeling. I sort of felt like playing God, but I definitely couldn't save 30 people. So I thought maybe we can save her. And we did. She stayed in the hospital about three days. This was in October of 1944. She stayed in the hospital for three days. The nurses and doctors all loved her, because she was helping with everything. And the third day she came to me, and she said, oh, I was discharged. I have to leave. But it's raining, and it's cold outside, and I wish I could stay another day. And I remember I went to the storage, where we had some stuff, and I stole a flannel pajama jacket for her. And I took it out somehow under my dress, or something, so nobody would see it. And I called her. I gave it to her. And I said, put these under your clothes, because it was really cold and rainy. Maybe, you know, it will help. And she said, oh, I just talked to the doctor, and I can stay another day.  And she was very happy about it. About half an hour later. Dr. Mengele came with his usual entourage, with the shouting and with the whistling, and we were all ordered outside of the barracks, all of us that worked there, doctors and nurses. And we were not allowed to step back in anymore. And they began the selection of the people of this hospital. They evacuated this hospital at this point. All-- we had 680-some people-- all but 80 were taken in trucks to the gas chambers, and my friend was one of them. Maybe she could have survived. But such was fate, I think. We were not allowed to step back in the hospital. But there were mothers who had a daughter there, maybe, for a couple of days, just to give her a little rest in the hospital. There were daughters who might have mothered their sisters' sisters. And they all were watching taking their loved ones, of course, in the trucks. One of the girls, a 20-year-old, very beautiful girl had her mother-- happened to have mother there. And she risked her life. She went back, and she asked Dr. Mengele to save her mother. He looked at her, and he asked, how old is your mother. She said, my mother is 40 years old. He looked at her again, and said, your mother lived enough, and sent her off to her death. I mean, he was a real monster. He took sheer delight in murdering as many people as he possibly could. He didn't just follow orders. It was his goal in life, and it was his big passion in life, I think, to murder Jews. After we witnessed all these, and we were told to just turn around, and go, we all had the feeling that we might go to the same destination. But, again, we didn't. We went to the other side of the tracks, where the so-called working camps were because they were taking people-- IG Farben Industry had a plant near Auschwitz, and some people were taken to work details from Auschwitz. We were taken there, and eventually, after about three weeks, we were taken away-- we were supposed to go into small working camps to work as nurses or some doctors in these little clinics where they had maybe first aid kits or dispense an aspirin. Really not more than that. They didn't have doctors in these clinics. Maybe some of the bigger ones did, but the small working camps didn't. 20 of us were taken. It was an unbelievable journey. We were guarded by seven SS men, 20 of us helpless women. We were given some decent clothes before we left. The reason for that-- and our heads were not shaved anymore. So I had like short hair after six months. The reason for being given decent clothes was that we were traveling in a regular train. We had our own compartment. There was nobody with us. But we changed trains at certain stations so they didn't want to show us off like [INAUDIBLE]. We were-- we looked OK-- like people. I guess the last time my cousin saved my life-- I had some kind of a rash that also came about after the measles. And it came and went. It was like little red spots mainly around my side, on my side of my stomach, and I just happened to have a couple when we were last checked out. Mengele checked us out before we left. Naked, of course. Everybody. And my cousin told me to tell him if he stops me that I do get these every time I eat-- we were given some kind of a cheese sometime-- some kind of a very sharp cheese-- and to tell him that I get it from cheese, which wasn't true, because it had no connection. But that gave him an idea that I'm just allergic to that, and it's and allergy, so he did let me go. That was, I guess, the last time she saved my life. Anyway, the journey was unbelievable, because after six months in Auschwitz, it was the first time we saw people, actually. And we couldn't talk to anybody. But we did see people. And we saw cities. They were all in pretty bad shape, because this was November of 1944. They were all bombed out. We stayed about eight days in Braunschweig, which was a non-Jewish women's camp, because from there, they took us to various camps where we were needed. I was taken to a small town, called Salzwedel -- S-A-L-Z-W-E-D-E-L. It's about 80 kilometers south of Hamburg, very close to another death camp or a big working camp, Bergen-Belsen. Basically another extermination camp. Salzwedel was a very small camp. Only 1,200 women. All but four Belgium ladies who must have gotten there by mistake. All the rest of them Jewish. I was supposed to work, of course, in this little clinic. But the nurse who already worked there wanted the job for her sister. So by the time I got there, I didn't have the job. I guess I was still the little shattered dummy that I was six months before. I really couldn't stand up for my rights. We didn't have any rights. And we worked in ammunition factory manufacturing bullets that was very close to the camp. I suppose the camp was built there for that purpose. We worked a-- 12-hour shifts, 6:00 in the morning to 6:00 at night, or 6:00 at night to 6:00 in the morning. At the very beginning, I worked in the so-called potato peeling and vegetable peeling room next to the kitchen, separate from the kitchen. The reason for that was because when I arrived alone, I guess I was sort of a celebrity, and the SS officer who was in charge of the camp, who was a sadist by the way, came to me, and he told me not to tell anybody about what is going on in Auschwitz, because these women left very early. They were there, like, from July, I think, of-- and they left immediately after they got to Auschwitz. And they weren't aware of really what is going on. And he actually gave me the order not to tell anybody. And I think to isolate me, he put me first to work in this potato peeling room, which was a wonderful place, because each of us got a boiled potato every day. We could boil a potato every day, which was such a treasure, and such unbelievable nourishment, that actually I didn't even eat my soup that we got every day. I gave that soup to a little Polish girl who worked in the factory. We worked longer hours than the factory. We worked probably 15 or 16 hours. And for that soup, every night when I get back to the barracks, she had a pot of hot water for me. It was winter, and we had a little stove in the barracks. She had a pot of hot water for me so I could wash at night when I got back, which was an absolute unbelievable luxury, but I didn't eat my soup. And we also could eat, even though those vegetables were of terrible quality, basically, they were fit for animals, not human beings, but they were fresh vegetables. So we could also eat that while we were peeling them so it was like heaven. But that heaven only lasted sometime in January. I got there early December. They needed everybody in the factories. So I worked in the factory with the rest of the people. I have to tell you about a good German at this point. There was this 24 year old, very simple, semi-educated SS woman who was in charge of the kitchen. We had a little girl-- her name was [INAUDIBLE]. She was from Poland. An 18 year old-- my age-- who worked also in the potato peeling room. And she was an epileptic. I never know how she made it and how they-- she probably just left the camp before they were discovered. She looked good. She did have these seizures. I've never seen anything like these before or after, but it is not a pretty sight. Whenever she had a seizure, we sent for Anne-Lise, this SS woman. She came in. She would, basically, pick her up, and kiss her, and, and comfort her, and you know, be there for her until the seizure was over. And she was wonderful. She was an SS woman, but she was just a wonderful person. She was never exposed to a death camp. She did not know what's going on, you know. She wasn't-- she joined the SS-- I don't know, probably that was the only job she could get. And she was one of the few good Germans. In the-- the person who told me not to tell people was-- if he caught women trying to steal potato peels or discarded rotten vegetables from the garbage bins, he would beat them up fiercely. I mean he was not one of the nice guys, definitely. But he wasn't-- he was in charge of the camp, because he lived there. There was an SS officer above him in rank, who did not live in the camp, nearby somewhere. He came in every day only for roll call, maybe for 15 minutes or half an hou. We had a roll call when we came back from the factory. As far as we knew, he didn't allow physical punishment while he was there. So while he was there, that half an hour or 15 minutes, nobody was beaten. But we really can thank him for our lives, because he did have standing orders to destroy all of us before our liberation. Basically, there wasn't any point to it to do it, because we were liberated by the Americans, which compared to being liberated by the Russians, was a much better deal for them. Two weeks before we were liberated-- I've left Auschwitz with a cousin of mine, who is the younger sister of this cousin who saved my life, and who's closer to my age, but we were separated in Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she knew where I went, but I didn't know where she went. I went first. About two weeks before liberation, everything was already pretty chaotic. We could see the Allied airplanes flying over, and-- it's interesting, you know, talking about that. We had air raids practically every night while I was in Salzwedel. This was 1945 already. And we knew it. About five minutes after 8 o'clock, we're going to go down to the shelter, so everybody started to go to the bathroom, like about a quarter to 8.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were in the factory. At night, we went to the shelter. But if we were in camp, we didn't have any shelter, of course. But somehow, I think, most of us weren't afraid. It was such a sure joy to know that our side is up there. That even though, you know, we could have been killed if they bombed, but they didn't-- nobody was afraid, even if we weren't-- and we were-- at the very end, we were just going out, and we were shouting, you know. I mean, we knew it, the end is close. Actually the SS left about a week before the liberation, because they thought that they're closed, but that they came back. And then those who were really bad, like this guy who used to beat people up, he left. He didn't want to be caught with us, and a few others did leave. But, again, about two weeks before liberation, all of a sudden I see this group of women walking in the camp. A large group. And I'm looking through a window. I was in the camp, I guess I was on the night shift that week. And my cousin was there. The one that I was separated from, Eva. So it was a real joy, because I didn't have-- having no sisters or brothers-- cousins were my closest relatives. So I jumped through a couple of windows, I think. And somebody stole my shirt that I just washed. I remembered because I left it behind me. But I was very happy to see her. Eventually, several thousand women were take-- but in our camp. Some Jewish and some not. That was a big group of French women prisoners, too, that were taken there. And several times there was a rumor that they're coming, they're coming. We'll be liberated. And it was just a rumor. And it didn't happen. But on April the 14th, it was a Saturday, and we had no reading material or anything, but somehow somebody gave me a book-- a German novel-- I don't know-- I found it somewhere-- maybe the SS who left some stuff behind. I don't remember. But I found the book. And I was lying on the earth, basically, by the fence, on the other side of the camp. It was a nice spring day, and I was reading. And there was no work in the factory the last week or so anymore. And somebody came again and shouted, they're coming, they're coming. For the first five minutes, I think, I didn't get up. But then I was running to the gate like everybody else. And our gates were open actually by French POWs. There was a POW camp on the other side of the town, and I guess they were liberated first, that side. And the French POWs, most of them Jewish guys, opened our gates. And then, of course, the American army was coming. The POWs had a lot more communication with the outside world than we did. And also there were the so-called free workers-- I mean, free, quote, unquote, 'free.' They were mainly. French I guess some Belgium and Dutch, too, who were forced to go work in Germany. And they had a lot more freedom than we did. They were also in camps, but they had some freedom of movement. We were isolated. We had no news because we were in-- you know, locked in camps. But while we worked in the factory, there were some French workers-- they're mechanics who worked-- and I remember one of them, Josef, was a French mechanic. And every time my machine broke down, and, of course, I did let it run broken down as long as I could get away with it. But after awhile, I couldn't so I had to call somebody to fix it. And he would come. And he would face my machine. We were not allowed to talk to each other. And he would talk to the machine. And he would tell the machine like in French, you know, [SPEAKING FRENCH], 60 kilometers from Paris or something. And then slowly each girl from around the machines would come to me after we left, and we would share the news. So we had a little bit to understand. Anyway, April 14th was probably one of the most beautiful days of my life, even though I was only incarcerated for 10 and 1/2 months, somehow you get so used to it--
